--- title: "Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies" subtitle: "Unit 5 FullProject" author: "Sydney Ahlstrom Sanders" format: html: self-contained: true page-layout: full title-block-banner: true toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-location: body number-sections: false html-math-method: katex code-fold: true code-summary: "Show the code" code-overflow: wrap code-copy: hover code-tools: source: false toggle: true caption: See code execute: warning: false --- ::: {#1aa8b3b1 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import polars as pl import numpy as np from lets_plot import * # add the additional libraries you need to import for ML here import requests import io from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True) ``` ::: ::: {#4ea18d00 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # import your data here using polars and the URL sw_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv" response = requests.get(sw_url) content = response.content.decode('latin1') start_index = content.find("RespondentID") clean_content = content[start_index:] sw = pl.read_csv(io.BytesIO(clean_content.encode('latin1')), truncate_ragged_lines=True) print(sw.columns) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ['RespondentID', 'email', 'seen_any', 'star_wars_fan', 'seen_episode_I', 'seen_episode_II', 'seen_episode_III', 'seen_episode_IV', 'seen_episode_V', 'seen_episode_VI', 'rank_episode_I', 'rank_episode_II', 'rank_episode_III', 'rank_episode_IV', 'rank_episode_V', 'rank_episode_VI', 'han_solo', 'luke_skywalker', 'princess_leia_organa', 'anakin_skywalker', 'obi_wan_kenobi', 'emperor_palpatine', 'darth_vader', 'lando_calrissian', 'boba_fett', 'C_3PO', 'R2_D2', 'jar_jar_binks', 'padme_amidala', 'yoda', 'who_shot_first', 'know_expanded_universe', 'expanded_universe_fan', 'star_trek_fan', 'gender', 'age', 'household_income', 'education', 'location'] ``` ::: ::: ## Executive Summary _The objective of this analysis was to process survey data from FiveThirtyEight regarding Star Wars fans to help enable targeted marketing and predictive modeling. I was asked to clean the dataset, extract any meaningful information, and build a model to predict high-income respondents. The results confirm that Star Wars survey data is a reliable source for behavioral insights. My recommendations would be to use the extracted names for Star Wars Day promotions, and to focus high income marketing efforts on the demographics that are identified in the feature importance results with the highest scores._ _Data Preparation: I successfully cleaned the "messy" survey dataset, extracting respondent names from email addresses and mapping categorical preferences to numeric scales for easier understanding._ _Machine Learning: I developed a Random Forest model to predict if a respondent earns at least $50,000 annually. The model achieved an accuracy of approximately 59.88%, identifying age and level of education as the most significant predictors of income (followed by their opinion on Jar Jar Binks)._ _STRETCH CHALLENGE: Yes, the stretch challenge was completed by recreating two of the original chart visualizations from the article and showing that the data used here matches the original findings, validating the dataset._ ## Methods ### Task 1 _The raw data contained metadata before the headers, which I bypassed using find RespondentID and io.BytesIO. To assist the marketing team, I used Regex to parse the email column into first name, last name, and a standardized lowercase email domain. As required, there is a table below showing the number of times each email domain is used among the respondents._ ::: {#6c67ae1a .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} sw_cleaned = sw.with_columns( name_part = pl.col("email").str.extract(r"^([^@]+)"), email_domain = pl.col("email").str.extract(r"@([^.]+)").str.to_lowercase().str.strip_chars() ).with_columns( # FIRST NAME first_name = pl.when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"\.")) .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([^.]+)")) .when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"_")) .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"_([a-zA-Z]+)")) .otherwise(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"([a-zA-Z]+)$")), # LAST NAME last_name = pl.when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"\.")) .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"\.([^@]+)")) .when(pl.col("name_part").str.contains(r"_")) .then(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)_")) .otherwise(pl.col("name_part").str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)")) ) # Verify print("Final Name Extraction") print(sw_cleaned.select(["email", "first_name", "last_name", "email_domain"]).head(5)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Final Name Extraction shape: (5, 4) в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ email в”Ҷ first_name в”Ҷ last_name в”Ҷ email_domain в”Ӯ в”Ӯ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ӯ в”Ӯ str в”Ҷ str в”Ҷ str в”Ҷ str в”Ӯ в•һв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Ў в”Ӯ atticus.kensington@GMAIL в”Ҷ atticus в”Ҷ kensington в”Ҷ gmail в”Ӯ в”Ӯ barrett_amelia@YAHOO в”Ҷ amelia в”Ҷ barrett в”Ҷ yahoo в”Ӯ в”Ӯ carrington54543jack@myorg в”Ҷ jack в”Ҷ carrington в”Ҷ myorg в”Ӯ в”Ӯ emma.miller@gmaiL в”Ҷ emma в”Ҷ miller в”Ҷ gmail в”Ӯ в”Ӯ brielle.ironwood@GMAIL в”Ҷ brielle в”Ҷ ironwood в”Ҷ gmail в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ ``` ::: ::: ::: {#6248f237 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} domain_counts = ( sw_cleaned .group_by("email_domain") .len() .sort("len", descending=True) ) print("Survey Respondents by Email Domain") print(domain_counts) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Survey Respondents by Email Domain shape: (3, 2) в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ email_domain в”Ҷ len в”Ӯ в”Ӯ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ӯ в”Ӯ str в”Ҷ u32 в”Ӯ в•һв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Ў в”Ӯ gmail в”Ҷ 707 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ myorg в”Ҷ 286 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ yahoo в”Ҷ 193 в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ ``` ::: ::: ### Task 2 _Per the client's request, I filtered the dataset to the 835 respondents who had seen at least one film by checking for non-null values across the episode columns. To prepare for machine learning, I converted categorical ranges (Age, Education, Income, and Favorability) into numeric scales from 1-5 other than age which is 1-4._ ::: {#ff49541e .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} sw_filtered = sw_cleaned.filter( pl.any_horizontal(pl.col("^seen_episode_.*$").is_not_null()) ) print(f"Filtered rows: {sw_filtered.height}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Filtered rows: 835 ``` ::: ::: ::: {#0020acca .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 1. Age Mapping age_map = { "18-29": 1, "30-44": 2, "45-60": 3, "> 60": 4 } # 2. Education Mapping edu_map = { "Less than high school degree": 1, "High school degree": 2, "Some college or Associate degree": 3, "Bachelor degree": 4, "Graduate degree": 5 } # 3. Income Mapping income_map = { "$0 - $24,999": 1, "$25,000 - $49,999": 2, "$50,000 - $99,999": 3, "$100,000 - $149,999": 4, "$150,000+": 5 } # 4. Favorability Mapping fav_map = { "Very unfavorably": 1, "Somewhat unfavorably": 2, "Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)": 3, "Unfamiliar (please leave blank)": 3, # Treating unfamiliar as neutral "Somewhat favorably": 4, "Very favorably": 5 } # Apply mappings and remove original columns sw_ml = sw_filtered.with_columns([ pl.col("age").replace_strict(age_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64).alias("age_num"), pl.col("education").replace_strict(edu_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64).alias("edu_num"), pl.col("household_income").replace_strict(income_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64).alias("income_num"), pl.col(["han_solo", "luke_skywalker", "princess_leia_organa", "anakin_skywalker", "obi_wan_kenobi", "emperor_palpatine", "darth_vader", "lando_calrissian", "boba_fett", "C_3PO", "R2_D2", "jar_jar_binks", "padme_amidala", "yoda"]) .replace_strict(fav_map, default=None).cast(pl.Int64) ]).drop(["age", "education", "household_income"]) # Preview print(sw_ml.select(["age_num", "edu_num", "income_num", "yoda", "han_solo"]).head()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` shape: (5, 5) в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ age_num в”Ҷ edu_num в”Ҷ income_num в”Ҷ yoda в”Ҷ han_solo в”Ӯ в”Ӯ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ӯ в”Ӯ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ӯ в•һв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Ў в”Ӯ 1 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ null в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 1 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ null в”Ҷ 4 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 1 в”Ҷ 3 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 1 в”Ҷ 3 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 1 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ ``` ::: ::: ::: {#984b3ae7 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Validation table to show the mapping worked validation_table = ( sw_ml.select([ "first_name", "age_num", "edu_num", "income_num", "han_solo", "yoda" ]).head(10) ) print("Final Data Excerpt") print(validation_table) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Final Data Excerpt shape: (10, 6) в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ first_name в”Ҷ age_num в”Ҷ edu_num в”Ҷ income_num в”Ҷ han_solo в”Ҷ yoda в”Ӯ в”Ӯ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ҷ --- в”Ӯ в”Ӯ str в”Ҷ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ҷ i64 в”Ӯ в•һв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Әв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•җв•Ў в”Ӯ atticus в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ null в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ jack в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ null в”Ӯ в”Ӯ emma в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 3 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ brielle в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 3 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ harper в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ caroline в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ null в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ harper в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ null в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ ryan в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 3 в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ gideon в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 3 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ 3 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в”Ӯ benjamin в”Ҷ 1 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 2 в”Ҷ 4 в”Ҷ 5 в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ ``` ::: ::: ### Task 3 _I finalized the data by dropping identifiers (Name, Email, RespondentID) and applying One-Hot Encoding to remaining categorical strings. Our target variable was defined as a binary indicator for Household Income $\ge$ $50,000. Essentially this just means I dropped any non-numeric values so the model could be trained appropriately, and then told it what to predict with the data it recieves._ ::: {#7ccc9d7f .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # 1. Create the Target Column sw_ml_ready = sw_ml.with_columns( target = pl.when(pl.col("income_num") >= 3).then(1).otherwise(0) ) # 2. Drop identifiers and original target source cols_to_drop = ["RespondentID", "first_name", "last_name", "email", "name_part", "income_num"] sw_ml_ready = sw_ml_ready.drop(cols_to_drop) # 3. One-Hot Encode sw_final = sw_ml_ready.to_dummies( columns=[col for col, dtype in sw_ml_ready.schema.items() if dtype == pl.String] ) # 4. Handle any remaining Nulls sw_final = sw_final.fill_null(0) print(f"Final feature count: {len(sw_final.columns)}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Final feature count: 67 ``` ::: ::: ## Results ### Model Description _I utilized a Random Forest Classifier with 100 estimators. This model is ideal for survey data as it captures interactions between features (for example, how Age and Education combined might predict Income) without assuming a linear relationship._ ### Model Accuracy _The model achieved an accuracy of approximately 59.88%. This model successfully exceeds the client's 55% accuracy threshold. It may seem like a lower score than what is generally hoped for, but when you think about how accurately it is able to predict a person's yearly income based off of their opinions on Star Wars films, it seems like a reasonable, and even impressive performance._ ::: {#ee725967 .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Separate Features X = sw_final.drop("target") y = sw_final.select("target") # Split data X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split( X.to_numpy(), y.to_numpy().ravel(), test_size=0.2, random_state=42 ) # Initialize and Train model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42) model.fit(X_train, y_train) # Predict and Evaluate y_pred = model.predict(X_test) accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred) print(f"Model Accuracy: {accuracy:.2%}") print("\nClassification Report:") print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Model Accuracy: 59.88% Classification Report: precision recall f1-score support 0 0.54 0.62 0.57 73 1 0.66 0.59 0.62 94 accuracy 0.60 167 macro avg 0.60 0.60 0.60 167 weighted avg 0.61 0.60 0.60 167 ``` ::: ::: ### Feature Importance _The following chart shows which factors were the strongest predictors of a respondent making over $50k. Higher scores indicate greater importance in the model's decision-making process._ ::: {#58619f5d .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} feature_importance_df = pl.DataFrame({ "feature": X.columns, "importance": model.feature_importances_ }).sort("importance", descending=True).head(10) p_importance = ( ggplot(feature_importance_df, aes(x=as_discrete('feature', order_by='importance'), y='importance')) + geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5') + coord_flip() + labs( title="Top 10 Predictors of Income >= $50k", subtitle="Key factors identifying high-income respondents", x="", y="Importance Score" ) + theme_minimal() ) p_importance.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Data Validation - Stretch Challenge _To ensure the cleaning didn't compromise the data integrity, I recreated two visuals from the FiveThirtyEight article. This shows that the data matches the original publication. Matching these values also confirms the dataset is ready for predictive modeling._ ::: {#bf8b34fe .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} seen_cols = [f"seen_episode_{i}" for i in ["I", "II", "III", "IV", "V", "VI"]] movie_labels = ["The Phantom Menace", "Attack of the Clones", "Revenge of the Sith", "A New Hope", "The Empire Strikes Back", "Return of the Jedi"] seen_data = ( sw_ml.select(seen_cols) .select([(pl.col(c).is_not_null().sum() / 835 * 100).alias(c) for c in seen_cols]) .unpivot(variable_name="movie", value_name="pct") .with_columns( movie_title = pl.col("movie").replace(dict(zip(seen_cols, movie_labels))) ) ) shot_first = ( sw_ml.group_by("who_shot_first") .len() .filter(pl.col("who_shot_first").is_not_null()) .with_columns(pct = pl.col("len") / pl.col("len").sum() * 100) ) ``` ::: ::: {#7bd86f92 .cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} seen_data = seen_data.with_columns( pct_label = (pl.col("pct").round(0).cast(pl.Int64).cast(pl.Utf8)) ) final_movie_order = [ "Return of the Jedi", "The Empire Strikes Back", "A New Hope", "Revenge of the Sith", "Attack of the Clones", "The Phantom Menace" ] p1 = (ggplot(seen_data, aes(x='movie_title', y='pct')) + geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5', width=0.45) + geom_text(aes(label='pct_label'), nudge_y=3.5, va="bottom", size=7) + coord_flip() + scale_x_discrete(limits=final_movie_order) + scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 105]) + labs(title="Which 'Star Wars' Movies Have You Seen?", subtitle="Of 835 respondents who have seen any film",x="", y="") + theme_minimal() + theme( axis_text_y = element_blank(), axis_ticks_y = element_blank(), panel_grid_major = element_blank(), panel_grid_minor = element_blank(), axis_text_x = element_text(size=14, color='#333333'), plot_title = element_text(face='bold', size=15), plot_subtitle = element_text(size=14, color='#555555'), panel_spacing_y = 0.05, )) p1.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {#c89656af .cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} shot_first = shot_first.with_columns( pct_label = (pl.col("pct").round(0).cast(pl.Int64).cast(pl.Utf8)) ) final_shot_order = [ "I don't understand this question", "Greedo", "Han" ] p2 = (ggplot(shot_first, aes(x='who_shot_first', y='pct')) + geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5', width=0.40) + geom_text(aes(label='pct_label'), nudge_y=3, hjust=0, size=7) + coord_flip() + scale_x_discrete(limits=final_shot_order) + scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 55]) + labs(title="Who Shot First?",subtitle="According to 835 Star Wars respondents", x="", y="") + theme_minimal() + theme( axis_text_y = element_blank(), axis_ticks_y = element_blank(), panel_grid_major = element_blank(), panel_grid_minor = element_blank(), axis_text_x = element_text(size=14, color='#333333'), plot_title = element_text(face='bold', size=15.5), plot_subtitle = element_text(size=14, color='#555555'), panel_spacing_y = 0.05, )) p2.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
